prompt: Defend a genuinely unpopular or unfashionable opinion that you actually hold. Not a strawman — something you'd stand by.
response:
I believe that many modern productivity techniques, like multitasking and constant connectivity, actually reduce productivity rather than enhance it. Despite the popular embrace of always-on work cultures, I think periods of deep, uninterrupted focus are irreplaceable for meaningful intellectual work.

The research supports this view: studies show that it can take 23 minutes to refocus after an interruption, and that multitasking often results in all tasks being performed poorly. Yet our work culture continues to reward the appearance of busy-ness over actual output.

This isn't a popular position in many professional environments where immediate responsiveness is valued above all. Suggesting that you'll check email just twice daily or declining meeting invites to protect focus time can be seen as uncooperative. But I stand by the view that our most valuable contributions typically emerge from sustained attention rather than fragmented effort.
